metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `CREATE` and `SNAPSHOT` to one of the two disposition buckets (or a third IMPOSSIBLE/ERROR bucket) — both are in `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (confirmed at `reducer/_version.py:65`) but absent from the plan's exhaustive enumeration.
findings.finding: The orphan disposition constant is asserted to cover 'ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified,' but reading `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` shows `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` contains `CREATE` and `SNAPSHOT` — neither appears in either the AUTO-RECOVER nor HUMAN-TRIAGE bucket in the plan.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section — 'Define two sets in code (enumerating ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified)'; AC line: 'Orphan disposition is a code constant covering ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third disposition: ERROR/IMPOSSIBLE = {CREATE, SNAPSHOT} — an orphan CREATE or SNAPSHOT is structurally anomalous and should be logged as HUMAN-TRIAGE with a specific note, not silently unclassified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend the orphan disposition constant spec to explicitly handle `CREATE` and `SNAPSHOT` (e.g., carve them out with a comment explaining they are structurally impossible as orphans), so the 'ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES' exhaustiveness claim is actually true.
findings.finding: The plan's orphan disposition claim that the two sets cover 'ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified' is false: `CREATE` and `SNAPSHOT` are in `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` but appear in neither AUTO-RECOVER nor HUMAN-TRIAGE. An executor implementing the constant as a strict exhaustive check against `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` will hit an unhandled case for these two types.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section — 'Define two sets in code (enumerating ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third set `NEVER_ORPHAN = {CREATE, SNAPSHOT}` (with rationale: CREATE is the ticket anchor and cannot sort before itself; SNAPSHOT is the compaction artifact that defines the horizon) and assert `AUTO_RECOVER | HUMAN_TRIAGE | NEVER_ORPHAN == KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add CREATE and SNAPSHOT to the orphan-disposition code constant (with explicit reasoning for each: CREATE maps to MISSING_CREATE handling so mark it SKIP/already-handled; SNAPSHOT-as-orphan is a distinct case — specify whether it routes to HUMAN-TRIAGE or a dedicated handler), so the constant provably covers all 17 KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES.
findings.finding: The plan claims the two orphan-disposition sets enumerate 'ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified,' but CREATE and SNAPSHOT are both in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (verified in reducer/_version.py) and appear in neither the AUTO-RECOVER nor the HUMAN-TRIAGE list. An orphaned CREATE would be a MISSING_CREATE fault (handled separately), but an orphaned SNAPSHOT (a SNAPSHOT file sorting before a newer SNAPSHOT but absent from the outer SNAPSHOT's source_event_uuids) is a real edge case with no assigned disposition, leaving the constant incomplete and the claim false.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section — 'Define two sets in code (enumerating ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third set SKIP_TYPES = {CREATE, SNAPSHOT} to the constant, with a comment explaining CREATE orphans are handled by the MISSING_CREATE fault class and SNAPSHOT-as-orphan is escalated to HUMAN-TRIAGE. Update the completeness assertion/test to check that AUTO_RECOVER | HUMAN_TRIAGE | SKIP_TYPES == KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add CREATE and SNAPSHOT to one of the two disposition sets (or a third INVALID/IMPOSSIBLE set with explicit rationale), so the union of all sets equals KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES exactly.
findings.finding: The plan's disposition enum claims to cover 'ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so none is unclassified,' but KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES in src/rebar/reducer/_version.py contains 17 members while the plan's two sets (AUTO-RECOVER + HUMAN-TRIAGE) together cover only 15 — 'CREATE' and 'SNAPSHOT' are absent from both sets.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section — AUTO-RECOVER and HUMAN-TRIAGE set definitions
findings.suggested_fix: CREATE almost certainly belongs in HUMAN-TRIAGE (a duplicate CREATE would be order-sensitive and dangerous to auto-replay). SNAPSHOT is internal to compaction and should arguably go in a new IMPOSSIBLE/STRUCTURAL set (an orphan SNAPSHOT is a structural anomaly, not an orphan event in the normal sense) with a dedicated log-and-skip handler. Document the rationale for each in the code constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document that `gh workflow disable` does not cancel queued/in-flight runs; add a complementary quiescence check (e.g. `gh run list --status queued --status in_progress --workflow reconcile-bridge.yml` polling to zero, or checking that `refs/reconciler/lock` is absent) before beginning any repair writes.
findings.finding: `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` is asserted as the reconciler pause mechanism that guarantees no in-flight pass writes to the store during the repair window, but this is an unverified capability: `gh workflow disable` prevents NEW scheduled/webhook triggers from being accepted but does NOT cancel or block already-queued or in-flight GHA runs. A run that was queued (e.g. from the 20-minute schedule) immediately before the disable command completes can still execute concurrently with the repair loop, defeating the safety isolation.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Reconciler pause: disable the `reconcile-bridge.yml` GHA schedule for the window via the GitHub API/CLI (`gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml`), verify no pass is mid-flight'
findings.suggested_fix: After `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml`, poll `gh run list --workflow reconcile-bridge.yml --status in_progress --status queued --json databaseId --jq 'length'` until it returns `0` (with a timeout/abort), OR cancel any found runs with `gh run cancel <id>`, before proceeding. Document this in the Safety section and add it to AC3.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete quiescence verification command (e.g., `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress --json status` polling until empty, or checking `refs/reconciler/lock` is absent) and add it as a required pre-repair gate before the first write, with a timeout and abort-if-not-quiescent behavior.
findings.finding: The reconciler pause mechanism (`gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml`) stops future scheduled triggers but does NOT cancel or wait for a currently-running GHA job — the `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: false` setting (confirmed in .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml line 53) means an in-flight pass runs to completion uninterrupted. The plan says 'verify no in-flight pass' but gives no concrete mechanism: there is a TOCTOU window between checking for in-flight passes and beginning the first destructive write where a new pass could theoretically start (if triggered before disable takes effect) or an existing pass completes and another starts. The plan provides no waiting/polling step to confirm quiescence before writes begin.
findings.location: Safety section — 'disable the reconcile-bridge.yml GHA schedule for the window via the GitHub API/CLI (gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml), verify no pass is mid-flight'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Before first write: poll `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress --json databaseId` until the result is empty (or timeout after T minutes and abort). Also confirm `refs/reconciler/lock` is absent via the ref-lock API before proceeding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` (and `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` if the fsck overview description changes) to the Scope as explicit edit targets, alongside the corresponding golden files.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` is the actual package-data source the CLI reads for per-command help, but the plan names only the mirror golden files (`tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` + `-h` variant) as edit targets for the help text update. Updating the goldens without updating the package-data source `fsck.txt` (and its sibling `overview.txt` if the overview line changes) would leave the CLI binary serving the old `Usage: rebar fsck [--output json]` help text — the goldens would then fail, not pass.
findings.location: Scope section — 'update the `fsck --help` golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` + the `-h` variant'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'update the `fsck --help` golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` + the `-h` variant' with 'update `src/rebar/_cli/help/fsck.txt` (the package data the CLI reads) AND the mirror golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` + `fsck__dashhelp.out`; if the fsck overview description changes, also update `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` and the 6 overview golden files in `tests/golden/cli_help/`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete check for 'no in-flight pass' (e.g., `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress | wc -l` == 0, or a named lock ref inspection) so AC3 is unambiguously verifiable.
findings.finding: 'Verified no in-flight pass' (AC3 and Safety section) has no specified proving command or mechanism — an executor cannot implement this check without guessing the approach.
findings.location: Safety section: 'verify no pass is mid-flight'; AC line 3: 'verified no in-flight pass'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Verify no in-flight pass via `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress --json databaseId | jq \'length == 0\'`; abort if non-zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test that injects a git push failure (e.g., via a hook or a read-only remote fixture) and asserts the repair run aborts immediately with no further file writes or commits after the failed push.
findings.finding: No test is specified for the push-failure → abort path: the plan asserts 'a push failure ABORTS + surfaces the error' as a safety invariant, but AC2 only rehearses rollback; there is no called-out test that simulates a git push rejection and asserts (a) the run halts immediately and (b) no further writes occur after the failed push.
findings.location: AC2 / Safety section ('push FAILS → ABORT the run')
findings.suggested_fix: In the fixture test: set up a local bare repo as the remote, add a pre-receive hook that rejects pushes after batch 1, run repair with --limit=<batch_size>, assert that (a) an error is surfaced, (b) no batch-2 writes exist on disk, and (c) the local HEAD matches the pre-tag OID.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback failure path to the Safety section: specify what to do if `git push --force-with-lease` on rollback is rejected (e.g., fetch origin, inspect the origin commits to confirm they are safe reconciler commits post-repair, then use `--force` with explicit human sign-off, or escalate to manual recovery), so the operator is never left without a documented next step.
findings.finding: The rollback procedure (`git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git push --force-with-lease`) can itself fail if origin has advanced since the pre-tag was set (e.g., another writer pushed while repairs were running and a push failure was in progress). The plan specifies what to do when a forward push fails (abort) but does not specify what to do when the rollback force-push fails, leaving the operator in an unresolved state with local history diverged from origin.
findings.location: Safety section — 'rollback = git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git push --force-with-lease'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit abort-on-failure guard around `gh workflow disable`: if the call fails (non-zero exit / API error), the repair must halt before any write and surface the error, rather than proceeding with reconciler potentially active.
findings.finding: If `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` itself fails (auth error, GitHub API unavailable, CLI not installed), the plan does not specify whether the repair should abort or proceed — leaving the reconciler potentially active during live writes.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Reconciler pause: disable the `reconcile-bridge.yml` GHA schedule … `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml`'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `gh workflow disable` call in a checked subprocess invocation; on any non-zero return code, raise an error and exit before the pre-tag is created. Optionally verify the disabled state with `gh workflow view reconcile-bridge.yml --json state` before proceeding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the error handling for `gh workflow disable` failure: specify whether a non-zero exit from the disable command aborts the entire repair run (recommended), and document the required token scope and pre-flight permission check.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` itself fails (e.g., insufficient token permissions, GitHub API rate limit, network timeout). A failure here leaves the repair in an ambiguous state: the reconciler may still be running, yet the repair proceeds.
findings.location: Safety section — 'disable the reconcile-bridge.yml GHA schedule for the window via the GitHub API/CLI'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight step that verifies the disable succeeded (e.g., `gh workflow view reconcile-bridge.yml --json state | jq -e '.state == "disabled"'`) and aborts with a clear error message if it did not, before creating the pre-tag or writing anything.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert explicitly what the store's complete file-system state is after rollback (post-`git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation`), including the fate of `.bak` files written during partially-completed ORPHAN_EVENT rebuilds — either guarantee they are always committed before a push (so git reset removes them) or add a post-rollback cleanup step that removes any `.bak` files, and add this assertion to the rollback-rehearsal fixture in AC2.
findings.finding: The plan specifies rollback via `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git push --force-with-lease` but never asserts what the persisted store state IS after rollback completes — specifically: (1) `.retired` renames are reversed by git reset (good), but (2) `.bak` files created during partially-executed ORPHAN_EVENT snapshot rebuilds (A2's path writes `<snap>.bak` before the clean round-trip) are written to the working tree and may not be tracked by git if the commit hadn't yet occurred, meaning `git reset --hard` would NOT remove them, leaving `.bak` files alongside the restored snapshots in an undefined state.
findings.location: Safety section — rollback path: `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git push --force-with-lease`; AC2 — 'rollback rehearsed on a fixture (post-rollback store == pre-tag state)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the repair loop: after A2's rebuild succeeds and `.bak` is deleted, include the `.bak` deletion in the same `git add -A` staging as the rebuilt snapshot before the batch commit, guaranteeing both the new snapshot and the deletion of `.bak` are atomic from git's perspective. Add to AC2: 'post-rollback, assert no `.bak` files exist in the tracker and no `.retired` files exist that were not present pre-tag.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a described exhaustiveness assertion (e.g., a test that asserts `AUTO_RECOVER | HUMAN_TRIAGE == set(KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES)` and `AUTO_RECOVER & HUMAN_TRIAGE == set()`) so AC4's 'covering ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES' is provably verified.
findings.finding: AC4 requires the orphan disposition constant to cover ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, but no proving command or exhaustiveness mechanism is described to verify completeness at test or import time.
findings.location: AC line 4: 'Orphan disposition is a code constant covering ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES; AUTO-RECOVER rebuilt, HUMAN-TRIAGE surfaced (not auto-rebuilt); tested.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test suite: `assert AUTO_RECOVER_TYPES | HUMAN_TRIAGE_TYPES == set(KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES)` and `assert AUTO_RECOVER_TYPES & HUMAN_TRIAGE_TYPES == set()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a synthetic-fixture test that injects a failure on one ticket mid-batch and asserts: (a) the loop continues to the next ticket, (b) the batch commits only the successfully-repaired tickets, and (c) the failed ticket is still flagged by a subsequent fsck re-run.
findings.finding: No test is specified for the per-ticket failure → skip-and-continue path: the plan states 'Per-ticket failure logs+skips (no batch abort)' but no AC or test fixture covers what happens when repair of one ticket raises an exception mid-batch — specifically whether the loop skips correctly, the batch still commits the successful tickets, and the failed ticket is re-attempted on the next run.
findings.location: Safety section ('Per-ticket failure logs+skips (no batch abort)') / AC5-AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact module path and function signature of A1's retire helper that A3 will call (e.g., `from rebar.reducer._cache import retire_event_file(path: str) -> None`), or add a note that A3 must be coordinated with A1's final symbol placement.
findings.finding: The plan says A3 'CALLS A1's retire helper' but never names the retire helper's module path, function name, or signature. A1 ticket (b306) itself leaves the location unresolved ('in `reducer/_cache.py` or a small util'). A3 has no way to import the helper without knowing where it will live.
findings.location: Repair per fault class section — 'call A1's retire helper to rename each still-present listed source to `.retired`'; Scope section — 'it CALLS A1's retire helper'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the A1 retire helper's fully-qualified name, call signature (arguments + types), return value, and exception contract in the plan so the A3 fsck.py call-site can be implemented and tested against a stub without A1 being complete.
findings.finding: The plan references 'A1's retire helper' as the mechanism for SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT repair but never specifies the function name, module path, signature, return type, or exception contract — leaving the processing protocol undefined for the call-site in fsck.py.
findings.location: Repair per fault class — SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT; Scope section ('CALLS A1's retire helper')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism and value of the 'N minutes' auto-re-enable failsafe (e.g., a GHA scheduled workflow that checks and re-enables if disabled for >T minutes, or a detached subprocess timer started before the repair run), and add a test or documented verification that it fires even when the repair process is killed.
findings.finding: The 'auto re-enable after N minutes' reconciler failsafe timer is underspecified: N is not defined, and the mechanism (a subprocess sleep+enable, a GHA-scheduled job, an OS timer) is not named. If the repair process crashes hard enough to skip the `finally` block, and no independent timer fires, the reconciler remains disabled indefinitely.
findings.location: Safety section — 'auto re-enable after N minutes or on repair-process exit'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a separate, independent GHA scheduled workflow (e.g., running every 15 minutes) that checks `gh workflow view reconcile-bridge.yml --json state` and re-enables it if it has been disabled for more than a configurable threshold. This is independent of the repair process's finally block and survives crashes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC1/AC6) that explicitly requires the `fsck --help` and `fsck -h` golden files to be updated and passing in CI after the new flags (`--repair`, `--dry-run`, `--limit`) are added.
findings.finding: The Scope section describes a concrete deliverable — updating golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and the `-h` variant — but no acceptance criterion covers it. This is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section: 'update the `fsck --help` golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` + the `-h` variant'; AC1–AC7 (none reference golden files)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'Golden files `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and the `-h` variant are updated to reflect `--repair`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit` flags, and CI asserts no diff.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or explicit ops-runbook acceptance step) that verifies the reconciler is re-enabled after a simulated repair-process crash — either by asserting the finally/atexit handler fires or by specifying a documented timeout-based re-enable mechanism with a tested trigger.
findings.finding: No test is specified for the reconciler re-enable failsafe: the plan requires the reconciler to be re-enabled 'in a finally/failsafe' even on crash, but no test asserts that a simulated repair-process crash leaves the reconciler re-enabled (or that the auto-timeout fires).
findings.location: AC3 / Safety section ('RE-ENABLE in a finally/failsafe')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact auth precondition for `gh workflow disable/enable` (e.g. `GH_TOKEN` must be a PAT with `workflow` scope or a GitHub App token with admin:actions, NOT a standard `GITHUB_TOKEN`), and add a pre-flight `gh auth status` / permission probe that aborts the repair before any writes if the token cannot disable workflows.
findings.finding: `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` requires the GitHub CLI `workflow` OAuth scope (or repo admin permission) on the token supplied to `gh` — this is NOT satisfied by the `actions: write` GHA permission already declared in `reconcile-bridge.yml`, and a standard `GITHUB_TOKEN` cannot disable workflows. The plan states no auth precondition (no GH_TOKEN env var, no PAT scope requirement, no `gh auth status` pre-check), so an implementor running `fsck --repair` with only a `GITHUB_TOKEN`-scoped credential will get a 422/403 from the GitHub API and the reconciler will not be paused — silently defeating the primary safety gate before a 2422-ticket destructive write.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Reconciler pause: disable the `reconcile-bridge.yml` GHA schedule for the window via the GitHub API/CLI (`gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml`)' and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Safety section: 'Pre-flight: assert `gh auth token --check workflow` succeeds (PAT with `workflow` scope required; a GITHUB_TOKEN with only `actions: write` is insufficient for disable/enable). Abort repair if the check fails.' Also add a concrete AC3 proving command: `gh auth status && gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml && gh workflow list --all | grep -q disabled`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'N minutes' with a concrete timeout value (or a named config constant with a default and its rationale), co-located with the failsafe re-enable specification.
findings.finding: The reconciler failsafe re-enable timeout is specified as a literal placeholder 'N minutes' — an executor cannot implement this without choosing a concrete value, and no guidance for selecting it is provided.
findings.location: Safety section — 'auto re-enable after N minutes or on repair-process exit'
findings.suggested_fix: Given --limit=200 batches with >=30s waits, a repair window of ~2 hours is a reasonable upper bound; a failsafe of 3 hours (10800s) with a configurable env-var REBAR_A3_RECONCILER_TIMEOUT_S would be concrete and auditable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the in-tool behavior for HUMAN-TRIAGE orphans: define the output format (e.g., a structured TRIAGE-NEEDED line per ticket), the exit code (e.g., exit 2 if any human-triage items remain), and whether the batch continues processing other tickets after encountering a HUMAN-TRIAGE orphan.
findings.finding: The in-tool behavior when a HUMAN-TRIAGE orphan is encountered during a live repair run is unspecified: the plan says these are 'surfaced, NOT auto-rebuilt' and 'decisions recorded in the PR / a linked ops doc', but does not define whether the tool prints a warning and continues the batch, exits non-zero, writes a triage file, or pauses for input. This makes the exit-point behavior ambiguous for operators.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section + AC line 4 — 'HUMAN-TRIAGE surfaced (not auto-rebuilt); tested'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'N minutes' with a concrete default timeout value (e.g., 30 minutes) so the failsafe re-enable is unambiguously implementable without a clarifying question.
findings.finding: The failsafe re-enable timeout uses the literal placeholder 'N minutes' — an executor cannot implement this without choosing a concrete value.
findings.location: Safety section: 're-enable in a `finally`/failsafe (auto re-enable after N minutes or on repair-process exit)'
findings.suggested_fix: State a concrete value: 'auto re-enable after 30 minutes or on repair-process exit, whichever comes first'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC6's 'MISSING_CREATE (incl. `.retired`)' parenthetical: specify whether the test verifies (a) that MISSING_CREATE detection still works when `.retired` files are present in the fixture, or (b) something else — and align the language with the Repair section's 'no automatic write' policy.
findings.finding: AC6 says MISSING_CREATE is tested 'incl. `.retired`', but the Repair section states MISSING_CREATE gets 'no automatic write; human triage' — making the meaning of the `.retired` parenthetical in the test criterion ambiguous (does the test verify that `.retired` presence affects detection, or that repair produces a `.retired` file?).
findings.location: AC6: 'MISSING_CREATE (incl. `.retired`) repair correctly'; Repair section: 'MISSING_CREATE (1): `fsck --repair` logs it, no automatic write; human triage'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC6's MISSING_CREATE clause to: 'MISSING_CREATE: repair logs and surfaces for triage (no file written), even when `.retired` siblings are present in the fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-evaluate REVERT's disposition: either move it to HUMAN-TRIAGE (safest, given its target-dependency) or explicitly specify how the rebuild handles REVERT-of-a-folded-target (e.g., if the target UUID appears in source_event_uuids, the SNAPSHOT compiled_state already reflects it, so the REVERT is safe to replay) and add a fixture test proving correct behavior for that case.
findings.finding: REVERT is listed in AUTO-RECOVER as 'additive/commutative,' but the existing reducer (process_revert in _processors.py) is order-sensitive: a REVERT carries a target_event_uuid and un-applies a specific prior event (e.g., un-archives on REVERT-of-ARCHIVED). An orphaned REVERT whose target event is absent from the ticket produces undefined reducer behavior on rebuild — the rebuild will encounter a REVERT referencing a UUID that was never applied (or was folded into the SNAPSHOT's compiled_state). This is not commutative: the effect depends on the presence and ordering of the target event.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section — 'AUTO-RECOVER (additive/commutative): COMMENT, LINK, UNLINK, TAG_DELTA, COMMITS, BRIDGE_ALERT, REVERT'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly how concurrent manual writes during the repair window are handled — either provide a technical enforcement mechanism (e.g., branch protection rule requiring PR-only writes during the window) or define a concrete recovery procedure (e.g., 'if fsck after the final batch still shows faults introduced by concurrent writes, re-run the repair for those tickets only'), so the dual-write window has a defined handling path rather than only a recoverability assertion.
findings.finding: The plan's dual-write window protection for manual writers relies entirely on a social/documentation hold-off ('Manual writers hold off (documented)') with no technical enforcement. If a manual push lands between two repair batches, new events can be appended to tickets that were already repaired in a prior batch, potentially re-introducing ORPHAN_EVENT faults (for a ticket whose snapshot was just rebuilt). The plan says 'stray write is recoverable' but provides no recovery path — fsck re-run would surface the new fault, but the repaired batch has already been pushed.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Manual writers hold off (documented); the batched+committed+pre-tagged design makes a stray write recoverable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the minimum GitHub token scope required for `gh workflow disable` + `git push` (e.g., `actions:write` + `contents:write`), specify the credential source (e.g., `GH_TOKEN` env var sourced from a secrets manager or `gh auth login`), and add a preflight check that aborts if the token scope exceeds the declared minimum.
findings.finding: The plan invokes `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` (GitHub Actions API) and `git push` but does not declare what credential/token scope is required or how it is provisioned, leaving the access posture of this privileged outbound path undeclared.
findings.location: Safety section — 'gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml' and 'git -C <tracker> push'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / credential scope' subsection to the Safety section: state that a PAT or GitHub App token with only `actions:write` + `contents:write` on the target repo is required; source it via `GH_TOKEN` env var (never hardcoded); add a startup check (`gh auth status --hostname github.com`) that aborts the run if the token is absent or has admin scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit fallback branch to the orphan routing decision rule for event types not in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (forward-compat unknown types), specifying the disposition (e.g., HUMAN-TRIAGE with a distinct log message) and include it in tests.
findings.finding: The orphan routing decision table has no fallback for an orphan event whose type is NOT in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (a forward-compat unknown type). The plan asserts the two sets cover 'ALL KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES' but is silent on unknown/future types.
findings.location: Orphan disposition section; Repair per fault class — ORPHAN_EVENT
findings.suggested_fix: Route unknown-type orphans to HUMAN-TRIAGE by default (safest: do not auto-rebuild from an event whose semantics are unknown to this binary), emitting a distinct warning that cites is_unknown_newer_type().

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete terminal proving command for AC7 (e.g., `rebar fsck 2>&1 | jq -e '[.faults[] | select(.status != "TRIAGED")] | length == 0'` or equivalent) and specify how HUMAN-TRIAGE items are marked as resolved in the fsck output so the 0-unresolved claim is machine-verifiable.
findings.finding: AC7 ('Live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults') is the terminal end-state of the entire ticket but has no concrete proving command specified; 'before/after counts recorded' is a documentation step, not a verifying command.
findings.location: AC line 7: 'Live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults (triaged+documented orphans count as resolved); before/after counts recorded.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named ops runbook or ADR (e.g., `docs/ops/live-store-repair.md` or an ADR entry) capturing the git pre-tag + batch-commit + reconciler-pause protocol so future repair runs have a canonical reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel live-store mutation protocol (git pre-tag, batched commit+push, reconciler pause/resume via GHA API) that constitutes a significant operational architectural decision, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or named ops runbook is planned to capture it — only a vague reference to 'decisions recorded in the PR / a linked ops doc'.
findings.location: Safety section; Scope section; AC line 7 ('triaged+documented orphans count as resolved')
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/ops/live-store-repair.md` covering: pre-tag creation and naming convention, batch-commit message format, reconciler disable/re-enable steps, rollback procedure, and how to interpret the orphan disposition constants. Reference it from the plan's Safety section and from `fsck.py` module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose inter-batch wait as a CLI flag (e.g., `--batch-wait-secs`, default 30) and document the basis for the default (e.g., 'chosen conservatively; tune down after dry-run confirms low store load').
findings.finding: The inter-batch wait of '>=30s' is stated as a prose constant with no derivation, no measurement plan, and no CLI flag to override it — making it effectively hardcoded and difficult to tune when the store size or host I/O characteristics differ.
findings.location: Safety section: 'Batched `--limit=200`, wait >=30s between'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--batch-wait-secs INTEGER [default: 30]` to `fsck --repair`, update the golden help files accordingly, and add a one-line comment in code citing the rationale (e.g., 'conservative default; override for faster hosts').

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
